segments.s539-g81: چو introduces a temporal clause meaning 'when'; این is the demonstrative 'this' (the thing just mentioned) and serves as the clause's subject. گردد (from گشت) means 'becomes' or 'is made' and completes the clause; از پاک مادر جدا literally 'separated from the pure mother' places the source (از پاک مادر) before the adjective/particle جدا 'apart', a common poetic ordering.
segments.s539-g82: بکن is an imperative 'do/make' addressed to the addressee. هرچ is an indefinite relative 'whatever'; it introduces the clause modified by فرمان دهد ('that the king gives/orders'), where دهد is the present (subjunctive/indicative) 3rd-person of دادن used to mean 'gives/orders'. پادشا (king) follows as the agent of دهد — classical Persian often places the title after the verb phrase.
segments.s539-g83: ز ره 'from the road' is a prepositional phrase (ز is the poetic form of از). باز شد is a compound where باز (back/again) plus شد (became/happened) conveys 'turned back' or 'returned'. موبد تیزویر is a title plus name in apposition after the verb, a typical poetic inversion where the subject follows the predicate.
segments.s539-g84: بگفت (he told/said) is the narrative past; آنچ ('that which') introduces a free relative clause which is completed by بشنید ('he heard'). با here marks the indirect object 'to/unto' (با اردشیر = 'to Ardashir'). Word order places the recipient after با and the content clause before it, as is common in classical Persian.
segments.s539-g85: بدو گفت = 'he said to him' (بدو = 'to him/unto it' as dative). زو نیز مشنو سخن is a negative imperative: زو ('from him' = about him) plus نیز ('also/even') and مشنو (negative imperative of شنیدن) = 'do not listen (to him) either'; سخن 'speech' is the object 'the words/speech'. The phrase is an admonition not to heed that person's words.
segments.s539-g86: کمند آر is an imperative sequence: کمند 'lasso/snare' (object) then آر, here functioning as an imperative 'bring/prepare' (classical terse imperative use). و is the coordinating 'and'. بادافره او بکن places بادافره (a compound or epithet) before او 'him' and then the imperative بکن 'do/make' — so 'and prepare a snare and do/make his بادافره' (i.e., act upon him with whatever بادافره denotes). The order (object – person – verb) is a poetic inversion of the imperative construction.
segments.s539-g25: تو از ما گسسته is a participial clause: تو (you) + از ما (from us) + گسسته (perfect participle ‘having separated/broken away’), so it means “you, having separated from us.” بدین گونه is an adverbial phrase meaning “in this manner” formed from the prepositional prefix ب‑ plus دین/دین‑form (here functioning as part of the fixed expression بدین = ‘in this’), and مهر is the noun ‘love/affection’ that the whole clause modifies.
segments.s539-g26: پسندد is a third‑person singular present/imperfect form meaning “does (he) approve/like” and here stands before its subject by poetic inversion. چنین is an adverb ‘thus/so.’ کردگار سپهر is the subject in apposition meaning ‘the Maker (God) of the heavens’ (کردگار = the Creator/God; سپهر = the sky/heavens). The whole phrase asks whether the heavenly Creator approves of such conduct.
segments.s539-g27: چو خواهی introduces a conditional: چو = ‘if’ (here), خواهی = ‘you wish/ desire.’ The clause که بانوی ایران شوی is the subordinate result/goal clause introduced by که: بانوی ایران شوی = ‘you become/are the lady of Iran’ (شوی = second/third‑person present/subjunctive ‘become’). The structure is ‘If you want that you become…’ = ‘If you wish to become…’.
segments.s539-g28: به گیتی here sets the sphere ‘in the world’ (به + گیتی). پسند is used as a noun ‘approval/being pleasing’ and دلیران is a genitive group ‘of the brave.’ شوی (become) is the verb applying to the subject of the previous clause: literally ‘become pleasing to the brave in the world.’ Word order places the prepositional phrase and noun phrase before the verb by poetic style.
segments.s539-g29: This is an imperative clause: بگیر is the imperative ‘take!’ The nouns before it (هلاهل چنین زهر هندی) are direct objects or coordinated objects: هلاهل (‘jingling ornaments’ or small trinkets) and زهر هندی (‘Indian poison’), with چنین ‘such’ qualifying them. The speaker commands the addressee to seize/receive these things.
segments.s539-g30: به کار آر is an imperative idiom ‘put into use / employ’ (به کار = to/into use; آر = bring/apply). یکپار is an adverb ‘all together/entirely,’ and بر اردشیر marks the target ‘against Ardashir’ (بر = against/on). The whole is a command to apply those things collectively against Ardashir.
segments.s539-g31: فرستاده آمد is a simple past clause: فرستاده ‘the messenger’ (subject) + آمد ‘came’ (past tense). بهنگام شام is an adverbial phrase ‘at the time of evening / at evening,’ modifying آمد; relative order is typical literary Persian.
segments.s539-g32: به دخت گرامی is the indirect object ‘to the noble/dear maiden’ (به = to; دخت = maiden/daughter; گرامی = honored). آن پیام is the direct object ‘that message.’ بداد here functions as a past verb ‘gave’ (classical compound form of دادن), so the whole clause means ‘he gave that message to the noble maiden.’ (Note: the provided sense list for بداد does not fit the verbal use, so a sense index is marked accordingly.)
segments.s539-g9: دو فرزند او is the subject phrase: دو (two) quantifies فرزند (children/sons) and او is the possessor. شد is the past of شدن used here to mark that they came to be/arrived (meaning 'went/ended up') in the location given by به هندوستان; word order places the locative phrase after the verb (archaic/classical word order is flexible).
segments.s539-g10: به هر نیک و بد is a prepositional phrase meaning 'in/through every good and bad (fortune)'; هر modifies the paired nouns نیک و بد. گشته is the perfect participle of شدن (here meaning 'have become' / 'became') and it predicates the state expressed by the adjective همداستان ('of one mind' / 'like‑minded'). The clause therefore says they became like-minded in all circumstances.
segments.s539-g11: دو ایدر literally 'the two here' — ایدر is an archaic/poetic adverb meaning 'here'. The locative phrase به زندان شاه اندرون places them 'within the king's prison' (به + place, زندان شاه = 'the king's prison', اندرون = 'inside'). Word order places the locative after the subject phrase.
segments.s539-g12: دو دیده پر از آب is a nominal phrase 'two eyes full of water' where پر از ('full of') takes از + noun; و links the next coordinated phrase دل پر ز خون, where پر ز (using poetic ز = از) likewise means 'full of'. The construction uses physical terms metaphorically (آب = tears; خون = blood).
segments.s539-g13: به هندوستان is a locative phrase 'in/at India'. بود is the past copula 'was'. مهتر پسر is a noun phrase where مهتر ('chief, principal') modifies پسر ('son' or 'youth'), so the clause states that the chief/leading son existed in India.
segments.s539-g14: که is the relative 'who/which/that' introducing identifying information. بهمن بدی here functions as a proper name (Bahman Badi). نام آن نامور is a formulaic tag: نام = 'name', آن = 'that/the', and نامور = 'renowned/famous' — 'the renowned one named Bahman Badi.'
segments.s539-g15: فرستاده‌ای is an indefinite noun form 'a messenger' (the -ای is an archaic indefinite/relational ending); جست is past of جستن here meaning 'sought' (not 'leapt'). با رای و هوش is an instrumental/qualifying phrase 'with counsel and intelligence', describing the desired qualities of the messenger. Word order places the qualifier after the verb.
segments.s539-g16: جوانی 'a youth' is the noun being described by the relative clause که دارد به گفتار گوش. که = 'who'. دارد is the present/persistent verb 'has' (here part of the idiom 'گوش داشتن به گفتار' = 'to lend an ear to speech' / 'to listen'); به گفتار = 'to speech/words'; گوش = 'ear' used figuratively as 'attention/listening'. The clause therefore characterizes the messenger as a young man who listens to words.
segments.s539-g17: چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when' starting a temporal clause. از پادشاهی is a genitive-like expression 'of/from the kingship' and ندید ایچ is a negative past-form clause: ندید (did not see) + ایچ (nothing) together mean 'saw nothing / saw no (share)'. بهر here functions as 'for' or 'a portion for' (i.e. 'no portion of the kingship for him'), so the whole segment means 'when he saw there was no part of the kingship for him.'
segments.s539-g18: بدو داد is an object (بدو 'to him') plus the past verb داد 'gave' with an omitted explicit giver (common in Persian narrative). ناگه is an adverb 'suddenly/unexpectedly', followed by یکی پاره زهر 'one piece of poison' (یکی = 'one/a', پاره = 'piece', زهر = 'poison'). The word order is typical SOV-leaning narrative where the indirect object and adverb precede the verb phrase.
segments.s539-g19: بدو گفت opens the speech with 'he said to him' (بدو = 'to him', گفت = past of گفتن 'said'). رو here is an imperative/directional 'go' (the older colloquial verb رَو), and پیش خواهر = 'before/in the presence of the sister'. بگوی is the jussive/imperfect-subjunctive form of گفتن used as an imperative 'say / tell'. Together the clause is a reported command: 'He said to him: go and tell (it) before the sister.'
segments.s539-g20: که introduces the content of what to say ('that ...'). از دشمن این مهربانی is a prepositional phrase 'this kindness from the enemy' and مجوی is the negative imperative/subjunctive 'do not seek / do not expect'. The clause therefore reports the content: 'that you should not seek this kindness from the enemy.'
segments.s539-g21: برادر دو داری is a simple possession statement with the numeral after the noun (common in classical Persian): 'you have two brothers' (برادر = brother, دو = two, داری = you have). به هندوستان marks location 'in/to India' and attaches to the clause as the place of those brothers.
segments.s539-g22: به رنج و بلا گشته همداستان is an adverbial-verb phrase: به رنج و بلا 'by/through suffering and calamity' (به used here as an instrumental/causal marker), گشته is the perfect/participial sense 'have become', and همداستان is a predicate adjective 'like-minded / of one mind'. Together it means 'they have, through suffering and calamity, become like-minded.'
segments.s539-g23: دو در بند و زندان شاه اردشیر lists location and captivity: دو 'the two' (subject) + در بند و زندان 'in fetters and prison' with a following genitive: شاه اردشیر 'of King Ardashir'. Word order places the locative phrase before the possessor phrase, a typical narrative structure describing the situation of 'the two.'
segments.s539-g24: پدر کشته و زنده خسته به تیر is a compact appositive/elliptical description: پدر کشته 'the father (is) slain' (کشته = 'killed/slain'), و زنده خسته 'and the living one (is) tired' (زنده used substantively), به تیر 'by/with an arrow' (به marks the instrument/agent). The verbs are elided (common in poetry) so participles and adjectives stand in for full clauses.
segments.s539-g73: بشد is an archaic/past-formative verb meaning “(it) became / (he) rose” and here functions as the past action 'rose (up)'. The sequence موبد (mōbed) follows the verb, so بشد موبد = 'the mobad rose'. وپیش او introduces the following phrase 'and before him' (وپیش = 'and before'), placing the locative phrase before the noun phrase دخت شاه ('the king's daughter'), a word order typical in Ferdowsi's older style.
segments.s539-g74: همی + past verb رفت signals a continuous/habitual past nuance often rendered 'was going' or simply past 'went' in translation; here it describes her movement. لرزان is a participial/adverbial modifier 'trembling' describing the manner; و links to the following nominal phrase دل پرگناه, where دل ('heart') takes the postpositive adjective پرگناه ('full of sin' / 'sinful').
segments.s539-g75: به موبد چنین گفت = 'said thus to the mobad': به marks the indirect object ('to the mobad'), چنین گفت is a fixed phrase 'said thus'. کای پرخرد is a vocative: کای = 'O' (address marker) and پرخرد = 'very wise', directly addressing the mobad.
segments.s539-g76: مرا و ترا = 'me and you' (object pronouns linked by و). روز هم بگذرد uses the 3rd-person present-subjunctive بگذرد in an optative/wish sense 'may the day pass'; هم adds 'also/too'. The clause is elliptical in that the verb governs the foregoing pronouns: 'may the day also pass for me and for you.' Note older segmentation in the gloss (م / ت) corresponds to مرا / ترا as objects.
segments.s539-g77: اگر introduces a conditional 'if'. کشت here means 'killing' (not cultivation) and خواهی is the 2nd-person present/future 'you wish/intend' (you want to). مرا را is the object 'me' (the gloss splits the pronoun into parts); ناگزیر 'inevitably/so be it' qualifies the consequence or tone of resignation. The clause is slightly elliptical—common in older Persian—leaving the consequence implied.
segments.s539-g78: یکی کودکی دارم = 'I have one/a certain child': یکی functions as 'one / a certain', کودکی here means 'a child' (not 'childhood'), and دارم is the present 'I have'. از اردشیر marks parentage 'by/from Ardashir' (از indicates origin/agent in this kinship sense). Word order is common Persian: numeral + noun + دارم, then the origin phrase.
segments.s539-g79: اگر sets a conditional/hypothetical. من سزایم = 'my due / my deserved punishment' with the possessive suffix embedded in سزایم. به خون ریختن is a verbal-nominal phrase 'to the shedding/spilling of blood' where به + verbal noun expresses the nature of the punishment. The whole fragment reads 'if my due is blood-shedding...'.
segments.s539-g80: ز دار بلند اندر آویختن = 'to be hanged from a tall gallows': ز (poetic 'from'), دار here means 'gallows' (not house), بلند 'high/tall' modifies دار, اندر is a locative/old-style preposition 'on/upon', and آویختن is the infinitival/verbal noun 'to hang / to be hung'. Together they give the specific form of execution.
segments.s539-g41: سوی + NP is a directional prepositional phrase 'toward (the) ...'. Here سوی دختر اردوان places the goal ('toward Ardavan's daughter') before the verb. شد is the past of شدن used intransitively ('went/turned/moved' or simply 'came/was'), and ز راه ('from the road') gives the origin of motion. The sentence omits an explicit subject (typical in Persian), so the verbs imply 'someone (a runner/servant) went/moved.'
segments.s539-g42: دوان is a present/past participle/verb meaning 'running, rushing' and here describes the action of the next noun. ماه چهره is a compound epithet 'moon‑faced' (i.e. beautifully faced). بشد is an older/poetic past form of شدن used like 'came/became' (here 'came'), and نزد + شاه means 'in the presence of / before the king.' Word order is verb/adverbial + epithet + verb + prepositional phrase.
segments.s539-g43: بیاورد is the simple past 'she/he brought'. جامی is an indefinite/partitive form of جام 'a cup/goblet' (the -ی can mark indefiniteness or 'a certain cup'). ز یاقوت زرد is a genitival/descritive phrase 'of yellow ruby' (a poetic description of the cup's material or color). Word order: verb → object → modifying phrase.
segments.s539-g44: پر از is an adjectival phrase 'full of ...' followed by the contents. شکر 'sugar' is literal. و simply links two items. پست here appears as a content word joined to 'sugar' (i.e. another ingredient), and با آب سرد 'with cold water' specifies the liquid accompaniment. Grammatically, the noun phrase 'پر از شکر و ... با آب سرد' modifies جامی (the goblet she brought).
segments.s539-g45: بیامیخت is the past tense 'he/she/it mixed' (poetic compound of آمد + آمیختن). با introduces the things mixed together ('with sugar and پست'), and زهر is the direct object 'poison'. The verb governs the following instrumental phrase; sequence shows the agent mixing poison into the sweet ingredients.
segments.s539-g46: که begins a subordinate clause 'that/so that'. بهمن is a proper name (here the person Bahman). مگر in classical Persian can introduce a wish/possibility ('perhaps' / 'that so that'), and یابد is the present/subjunctive (here optative) 'may obtain/attain/receive'. از کام بهر literally 'from (her) desire for advantage' — از کام = 'from desire/pleasure' and بهر = 'for/so that'. Overall the clause expresses purpose or hoped-for result ('that Bahman might gain satisfaction').
segments.s539-g47: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'. بگرفت is the past 'seized, grabbed'. شاه اردشیر is the subject 'King Ardashir'. آن به دست literally 'that into (his) hand' — به دست here marks the location/recipient of the seizing (i.e. he seized that into his hand). Word order is typical classical Persian: temporal particle → verb → subject → object phrase.
segments.s539-g48: ز دستش 'from his hand' (ز = 'from', دست = 'hand', ـش = possessive 'his'). بیفتاد is the past 'fell (down)'. و = 'and' links the two verbs. بشکست is the past 'it broke' (intransitive/passive sense 'was broken' or 'broke'). پست here is attached as a final descriptor of what broke; the exact nuance is context-dependent (poetic/derogatory or a material name).
segments.s539-g65: چه here is an exclamatory/interrogative 'what' (How/what a ...). بادافره‌ست is a poetic compound meaning 'is wind‑borne' or 'caused by a gust' (the copula -ست marks a present/state). این is the demonstrative 'this' pointing to the thing just mentioned. برآورده is a past/participial notion 'that which has been brought forth/caused' and را is the definite/object marker attaching to the whole NP (it makes the phrase definite/emphatic). The word order is slightly inverted for poetic effect but functions as a nominal phrase marked by را before the next clause.
segments.s539-g66: چه again asks 'what/how' (here introducing the question 'what shall we do'). سازیم is 1st‑person plural present/subjunctive/optative 'shall we do / what can we do' (used to ask for a remedy). درمان is the noun 'remedy/cure' and خودکرده is a compound adjective literally 'self‑made' used here as 'self‑inflicted' (i.e. the harm he brought on himself). را is the object marker showing درمانِ خودکرده is the object of سازیم; in Persian the object often appears before the verb and carries را.
segments.s539-g67: چنین داد پاسخ is an inversion meaning 'he gave such an answer / he answered thus' (چنین 'thus', داد 'gave', پاسخ 'answer'). که introduces the content clause ('that …'). مهترپرست is a compound meaning 'sycophant / over‑reverent follower' and is the subject of the following clause.
segments.s539-g68: چو is the temporal/concessive 'when' here. یازد is an archaic/epic verb meaning 'seizes / makes a move / attacks' (3rd singular in narrative). بجان is a prepositional phrase 'by the life of' (i.e. target = the sovereign's life), جهاندار is 'the ruler/sovereign', and دست 'hand' is literal/figurative 'hand against'—together 'lays a hand at the sovereign's life' (attempts violence).
segments.s539-g69: سرش is 'his head' (سر 'head' + enclitic possessive -ش 'his'). بر گنه = 'on account of the sin / for the sin' (بر used in the sense 'on/upon' indicating cause). بباید expresses necessity 'it must / ought to' and برید (here infinitive/past stem of بریدن) is the verbal base 'be cut off / cut' — together the construction conveys 'his head ought to be cut off for the sin.' Note the classical ordering and the impersonal/modal use of باید.
segments.s539-g70: کسی here functions as 'anyone/if anyone' (indefinite subject). پند is 'advice/counsel'. گوید is used as 'says/offers' (introducing the hypothetical clause), and نباید شنید is an impersonal negative obligation 'it must not be heard / one should not listen' (نباید = must not; شنید = hear). The whole means 'if anyone offers advice, one must not heed it.'
segments.s539-g71: بفرمود is an honorific/epic past tense 'he commanded/said' (polite form). کز functions here as a preposition/relative 'concerning / about' and introduces the topic 'دختر اردوان' ('Ardavan's daughter' — daughter in genitive relation to the name اردوان).
segments.s539-g72: چنان کن is an imperative 'do so / do thus' (چنان 'so', کن imperative of کردن). که introduces the purpose/result clause 'so that...'. هرگز نبیند is a negative optative/subjunctive 'may never see' (هرگز 'never', نبیند subjunctive/negative). روان is used poetically here to mean 'the living/man' or 'him alive' (i.e. 'see him alive'); classical Persian often uses روان in such stylistic senses.
segments.s539-g49: شد is the past/resultative verb 'became' placed at the clause start (classical/poetic word order). آن پادشا بچه is a noun phrase 'that king's child' with the determiner آن preceding the head; لرزان is a participial/adjectival complement meaning 'trembling' and ز + بیم gives the cause 'from/because of fear'.
segments.s539-g50: هم‌اندر زمان is an adverbial phrase 'at the same time' (هم‌اندر = 'together/at the same'), followed by شد 'became'. دلش is دل + enclitic ش 'his heart', and به دو نیم literally 'into two halves' — 'دلش به دو نیم شد' is a resultative construction meaning 'his heart was split in two'.
segments.s539-g51: جهاندار is a title 'the sovereign'. زان (زن + آن) = 'from/for that reason' links back to the prior tremble as cause. لرزه is the tremor/fear; شد is 'became'; بدگمان is a predicate adjective 'suspicious/distrustful'. The clause thus means the ruler became distrustful on account of that tremor.
segments.s539-g52: پراندیشه is a compound adjective 'full of thought / pensive' modifying the sovereign; از here is used as 'concerning / about' (introducing the theme), and گردش آسمان = 'the turning/rotation of the sky' — a poetic expression for the movement of fate/heaven. The whole phrase says he was deeply thoughtful/worried about the heavens' turning.
segments.s539-g53: بفرمود is the honorific/polite past 'he ordered/commanded'. تا introduces a subordinate clause with purpose/content ('that ...'). خانگی modifies مرغ: 'a domestic bird'. چار is a numeral placed after the noun in poetic word order (here 'four'). The phrase sets up what he ordered to be brought.
segments.s539-g54: پرستنده is a noun/adjective 'devotee/attendant' (here describing companions/attendants of the bird). آرد is the classical/persian-present-subjunctive form of آوردن used after تا to express 'that they bring' (literally 'bring' in dependent clause). بر + شهریار = 'before/to the king' (بر here = toward/to).
segments.s539-g55: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. آن مرغ = 'that bird'. بر پست, with بر = 'on/upon' and پست used as the place ('the low post/platform' in context). بگذاشتند is the simple past 'they placed/put'. The clause narrates the placing action.
segments.s539-g56: گمانی is 'a supposition/omen'; همی is an emphatic/progressive particle here ('at once/indeed'); خیره is 'astonished' or 'dazed' describing their state, and پنداشتند is the past 'they supposed/imagined'. Together it means they immediately regarded it as an omen and were amazed.
segments.s539-g57: هم‌انگاه = adverb meaning 'immediately' (precedes the verb phrase). مرغ آن بخورد و بمرد is a compact past narrative: مرغ آن بخورد uses بخورد (here = 'ate' — simple past sense) with آن pointing to the thing the bird was given; و connects the two verbs; بمرد is a past/declarative 'died' (classical b‑prefixed past). The clause reports two sequential actions: the bird ate it and then died.
segments.s539-g58: گمان بردن is an idiom meaning 'to suppose/think' (گمان + بردن where بردن here = 'take/hold [an opinion]'). از راه نیکی ببرد is a content clause indicating what they supposed: از = 'from' (drawing an inference), راه نیکی = 'the way/road of goodness' (metaphorical 'a good end'), and ببرد (third person perfective/simple past sense of بردن) expresses 'he had taken/been carried along that way' — i.e. 'had departed in a good manner.'
segments.s539-g59: بفرمود is a courtly/past verb 'he commanded/said' (honorific narrative). تا here introduces the subordinate clause of purpose/command 'so that / that', followed by the two coordinated nouns موبد و کدخدای (the mobed, i.e. Zoroastrian priest, and the village headman).
segments.s539-g60: بیامد is the past narrative 'came'. بر is the preposition 'to/before' in the sense of approaching/coming before someone; خسرو here denotes the sovereign/king; پاک‌رای is an epithet 'pure‑minded/virtuous' modifying خسرو. Together: 'came before King Pakrāy.'
segments.s539-g61: ز is the poetic preposition 'from' (introducing the source of inquiry). دستور ایران is a fixed phrase here meaning the court/council or established order of Iran (دستور used in the sense of the polity/arrangement), and بپرسید is the past narrative 'he asked'. شاه is the subject 'the king' placed after the verb in classical word order.
segments.s539-g62: که introduces the content clause ('what/that'), بدخواه را is the direct object 'the ill‑wisher' marked by را, برنشانی is an infinitival/nominal form meaning 'to set/affix/mark' (here 'what to do about/ how to treat' the ill‑wisher), and به گاه means 'at the right time' (به = 'at/for', گاه = 'time/occasion'). The clause asks how/when to deal with the ill‑wisher.
segments.s539-g63: شود is future/passive auxiliary 'will become' (classical subjunctive/impersonal future). در نوازش = 'in/through caressing/flattery' (نوازش = affection/caress used figuratively), بران‌گونه = 'in that manner/so' (an adverbial modifier), مست = 'intoxicated' (literal or figurative 'drunk' on praise). Together: 'he will become intoxicated by such flattery.'
segments.s539-g64: که introduces the result clause 'so that'. بیهوده = 'in vain', یازد is a classical/poetic verb here meaning 'breaks forth/rushes' (from یاختن), به جان تو = 'upon your life' (به = 'upon/toward', جان = 'life'), دست = 'hand' used idiomatically for 'lay hands on/attack'. The whole clause: 'so that he will in vain rush to lay hands on your life.'
segments.s539-g1: بدانگه (thereupon) + که introduces a subordinate clause ('when/that'); the clause has the verb in an archaic past form. شاه اردوان را is a nominative phrase with the object marker را on اردوان (here marking the definite object): the verb بکشت is an old past form meaning 'killed'. The whole segment reads 'thereupon/when he killed King Ardavān.'
segments.s539-g2: ز خون وی is an ablative/causal prepositional phrase 'from/by his blood' (ز = 'from'); آورد is a simple past 'brought' (here transitive) and گیتی به مشت is the object phrase 'the world into (his) fist' with به + locative/goal meaning 'into'. The clause thus states the result: 'he brought the world into his fist from his blood.'
segments.s539-g3: بدان here functions as a prepositional/relational prefix meaning 'with/regarding that'; فر 'farr' = divine/royal glory and اورند is a proper name or epithet paired with فر. The segment places King Ardashir in the context of that glory: 'with that glory and Orvand, King Ardashir...'.
segments.s539-g4: شده + شادمان is a perfect/resultant construction: 'has become joyful' or 'was made joyful.' مرد is a general noun 'man/people' and برنا و پیر are contrasting adjectives 'young and old'; the segment lists the people affected: 'he became joyful — men, both young and old.'
segments.s539-g5: که introduces the clause; بنوشت here is an archaic/poetic form whose lexical sense in the provided list does not match the usage in context (it functions to indicate the ending/recording of the previous injustice). بیدادی modifies اردوان, meaning 'oppressive/tyrannical Ardavān' or 'Ardavān's injustice.' The segment signals that Ardavān's wrongdoing was in view/was dealt with.
segments.s539-g6: ز here is causal 'because/since/by'; داد means 'justice' (not the verb 'gave' here) and وی 'his' refers to the just ruler whose acts follow; آبادتر شد is the comparative 'became more prosperous' and جهان 'the world' is the subject affected. Together: 'by his justice the world became more prosperous.'
segments.s539-g7: چنو is an archaic connective 'thus/when/so' introducing the circumstance. کشته شد is a passive past 'was killed.' دخترش را with را marks the definite object 'his daughter.' بخواست is an archaic past/hortative meaning 'he desired/commanded' (here: 'he had it done' or 'he ordered it'). The clause says that his daughter was killed at his bidding.
segments.s539-g8: بدان (ب + دان) here functions adverbially 'by this/for that' and تا introduces purpose 'so that/in order that'; بگوید is a subjunctive 'he may say / (someone) may tell' (the provided sense list did not include the verbal sense so it is treated as the subjunctive of گفتن), که introduces the content clause, گنجش (from گنج) = 'treasure', کجاست = 'where is'. The whole segment expresses purpose: 'so that he may tell where the treasure is.'
segments.s539-g33: ورا is a prepositional/accusative pronoun 'to/for her' placed before the noun phrases for emphasis. جان و دل are two coordinated internal objects ('life and heart') and دل follows جان joined by و. بر برادر is a prepositional phrase 'toward/for (her) brother' (بر = toward/to), and بسوخت is the simple past 3rd-person singular verb (بسوخت, 'burned/was burned') that predicates the whole clause. Word order here is emphatic: indirect object/pronoun + coordinated inner objects + adpositional phrase + past verb.
segments.s539-g34: به کردار is an adverbial prepositional phrase 'in the manner of / by the disposition of' (به used adverbially). کرد ار ('کردار') means 'manner/deed' and modifies the following simile, آتش is the noun 'fire', producing the simile 'in the manner of fire'. رخش (Rakhsh) is the subject/name introduced after the simile, and برفروخت is a past-form verb meaning 'kindled / became inflamed' (here intransitive of sudden arousal). The clause therefore reads as a comparative: Rakhsh, like a fire, was kindled/flamming.
segments.s539-g35: ز اندوه is a causal/ablative phrase 'out of/ because of sorrow' (poetic ز = از). بستد is a past 3rd-person singular of بستن 'to close / bind / hold back' and is the verb of the clause. گرانمایه here functions as the subject 'the noble/precious one' (nounal adjective placed after the verb, a common poetic word order). زهر is the direct object 'poison'. Taken together: 'Because of sorrow the noble one shut up/held back the poison.'
segments.s539-g36: بدان بد is a compact phrase 'to/with that evil' (ب + دان forming the demonstrative-like/pointing phrase + بد 'evil/bad') and introduces the ensuing purposive/result clause. که is the subordinating conjunction 'that / so that'. بردارد here is a present-form used optatively/subjunctively ('may take / might remove') introducing the intended result or motive. از کام بهر is a prepositional complement 'from desire for (the sake of) advantage' (از = from, کام = desire, بهر = for/for the sake of). Grammatically the clause expresses the evil intention/purpose: 'with that evil — that he might take away from desire for his advantage.'
segments.s539-g37: چنان بد که is a correlative/comparative opener 'so (bad/strong) that...' introducing the following situation; here it simply sets the preceding state and leads into the temporal clause. یک روز is a temporal adverbial 'one day'. شاه اردشیر is the subject phrase 'King Ardashir' (title + name). Word order places the setting formula and time before the main action.
segments.s539-g38: به نخچیر is an adverbial phrase 'for the hunt / in hunting' (به + نخچیر). بر is the preposition 'at / against / toward' governing گور; here گور means the animal 'onager' (wild ass) rather than 'grave' because of the hunting context. بگشاد is the past form (ب‑ + گشاد) used as 'loosed / let fly' (classical poetic perfective), and تیر is the direct object 'arrow' following the verb — a common verb‑then‑object word order in older poetry. Together: 'in hunting he loosed an arrow at an onager.'
segments.s539-g39: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'. بگذشت is the past verb 'passed' referring to elapsed time. نیمی ز روز دراز is the temporal object 'half of the long day' (نیمی = half, ز = of/from, روز دراز = long day); the noun phrase follows the verb for poetic emphasis. The whole clause reads 'when half of the long day had passed.'
segments.s539-g40: سپهبد is the subject 'the spahbed' (a high military commander). ز نخچیرگه is a prepositional phrase 'from the hunting-ground' (ز = from, نخچیرگه = hunting place). گشت باز is a compound past meaning 'turned back / returned' (گشت = turned/became, باز = back/again). Typical word order here is subject + adpositional phrase + verb phrase.

sentences.s539-s41: When this becomes separated from the pure mother, do whatever the king commands.
sentences.s539-s42: From the road returned the mobed Tizvir; he told Ardashir what he had heard.
sentences.s539-s43: He said to him, 'Do not listen to that man either; set a snare and do (act on) his badāfareh.'
sentences.s539-s13: You, having broken away from us, do you favor love in this way — does the Maker of the heavens approve of such?
sentences.s539-s14: If you wish to become the lady of Iran, become pleasing to the brave in the world.
sentences.s539-s15: Take such halahel and Indian poison; put them all into use against Ardashir.
sentences.s539-s16: A messenger came at evening and gave that message to the noble maiden.
sentences.s539-s5: His two children went to India and, through good and bad, became of one mind.
sentences.s539-s6: Those two were inside the king's prison: eyes full of water (tears) and hearts full of blood.
sentences.s539-s7: In India was the chief son, the renowned one named Bahman Badi.
sentences.s539-s8: He sought a messenger — one with counsel and intelligence — a young man who has an ear for speech.
sentences.s539-s9: When he saw there was no share of the kingship for him, suddenly someone gave him a piece of poison.
sentences.s539-s10: He said to him, 'Go before the sister and tell her that you should not seek this kindness from the enemy.'
sentences.s539-s11: You have two brothers in India; through hardship and calamity they have become like-minded.
sentences.s539-s12: The two are in fetters and in the prison of King Ardashir; their father is slain, and the living one is weary from an arrow.
sentences.s539-s37: The mobad rose up, and before him the king's daughter walked, trembling and with a heart full of sin.
sentences.s539-s38: She said to the mobad thus: 'O very-wise one, may the day pass for both me and you as well.'
sentences.s539-s39: If you insist on killing me, so be it; I have one child by Ardashir.
sentences.s539-s40: If my due is the shedding of blood and being hung from a high gallows.
sentences.s539-s21: From the road a runner went toward Ardavan's daughter; the moon‑faced maiden rushed and came before the king.
sentences.s539-s22: She brought a goblet of yellow ruby, full of sugar and پست, with cold water.
sentences.s539-s23: She mixed poison with sugar and پست, so that Bahman might perhaps gain pleasure from it.
sentences.s539-s24: When King Ardashir seized that in his hand, it fell from his hand and the (vessel) broke.
sentences.s539-s33: What gust has blown this upon him? What can we do to remedy the harm he has brought upon himself?
sentences.s539-s34: He answered thus: that when a sycophant lays his hand at the life of the sovereign...
sentences.s539-s35: His head should be cut off for the sin; if anyone gives counsel, it must not be heard.
sentences.s539-s36: He commanded, concerning Ardavan's daughter, 'Do this so that she may never see him alive.'
sentences.s539-s25: That monarch's child became trembling with fear; at the same moment his heart split in two.
sentences.s539-s26: The sovereign, because of that trembling, became distrustful and pensive about the turning of the heavens.
sentences.s539-s27: He commanded that they bring a domestic bird (with four attendants) before the king.
sentences.s539-s28: When they placed that bird on the post, they at once took it as a sign and stood astonished.
sentences.s539-s29: At once that bird ate it and died; they took it as a sign that he had gone by a good way.
sentences.s539-s30: He ordered that the mobed and the village‑head come before Khosrow Pakrāy.
sentences.s539-s31: The king asked (the matter) of Iran's council/court what should be done about the ill‑wisher at the proper time.
sentences.s539-s32: He will be so intoxicated by such flattery that he will, in vain, rush upon your life.
sentences.s539-s1: Then, when he killed King Ardavān, from his blood he brought the world into his fist.
sentences.s539-s2: With that glory and Orvand, King Ardashir was joyful — men, both young and old.
sentences.s539-s3: When Ardavān's oppression was ended, by his (the ruler's) justice the world became more prosperous.
sentences.s539-s4: Thus, when his daughter was killed, he ordered it so that (by that) he might make (someone) say where the treasure is.
sentences.s539-s17: Her life and heart burned for her brother; like a flame, Rakhsh flared up.
sentences.s539-s18: Out of sorrow the noble one kept the poison sealed; with that evil (design) — namely that he might take from desire for his own advantage.
sentences.s539-s19: So (it was) that one day King Ardashir, while hunting, loosed an arrow at an onager.
sentences.s539-s20: When half of the long day had passed, the spahbed returned from the hunting-ground.
